content.text: Whether an agent can see the entire state of the world at once — or only a partial snapshot — is one of the most consequential design dimensions in agentic systems. This distinction directly determines how much internal state the agent needs to maintain and which action-selection strategies are appropriate.

attrs.alt: Fully vs Partially Observable Environments — side-by-side comparison

content.text: when the agent's sensors give it complete, accurate access to the world state at every time step. No variables are hidden; no inference about unseen state is required.
— the agent acts on current data, not memory or inference.
— the current percept is sufficient to determine the optimal action.
— a simple reflex or table-lookup agent can be optimal.
Classic examples include a chess engine (the full board is always visible), a thermostat (it reads the exact temperature), and grid-based Pac-Man when the full map is rendered.

class FullyObservableAgent:
    """A fully observable environment exposes the complete state each step."""

    def __init__(self, name: str):
        self.name = name

    def act(self, state: dict) -> str:
        """
        No belief state needed — the complete world state is given.
        This works only when the environment is fully observable.
        """
        # Direct table look-up; no uncertainty handling
        if state["temperature"] < 18:
            return "heat_on"
        if state["temperature"] > 24:
            return "cool_on"
        return "idle"


# Thermostat world: full state delivered at every tick
thermostat = FullyObservableAgent("Thermostat")
print(thermostat.act({"temperature": 16, "humidity": 55}))  # "heat_on"
print(thermostat.act({"temperature": 21, "humidity": 60}))  # "idle"
, not an agent property. An agent built for a fully observable environment will break if you later introduce hidden state — it has no mechanism to handle what it cannot see.
when sensors cover only a subset of the world state. The agent cannot directly know hidden variables and must reason about them indirectly. Most real-world environments are partially observable:
— the agent sees only its own hand and community cards, not opponents' cards.
— the robot's camera covers a limited field of view; walls around corners are not perceived until approached.
— other vehicles' intentions, occluded objects, and far-ahead traffic are not directly sensed.
— the agent only knows what was said in context; user intent and world facts outside the conversation are hidden.
When the environment is partially observable, the agent must maintain a
— an internal estimate of the current world state based on all prior percepts. The belief state bridges what the agent
Key operations on a belief state:
— incorporate a new sensor reading, weighted by how reliable that sensor is.
— reduce confidence over time. A belief that was accurate yesterday may be stale today.
— identify which variables the agent is least confident about, guiding information-gathering actions.
A partially observable agent wraps action selection around its belief state rather than directly around sensor input. Uncertainty triggers
(e.g., scanning, querying, asking) before committing to goal-directed moves:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field


@dataclass
class PartiallyObservableAgent:
    """
    An agent that operates in a partially observable environment.
    It maintains a belief state and uses it to make decisions.
    """

    name: str
    belief: BeliefState = field(default_factory=BeliefState)

    def perceive(self, sensor_data: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
        """Update beliefs from sensor readings."""
        for key, (value, confidence) in sensor_data.items():
            self.belief.update(key, value, confidence)

    def act(self) -> str:
        """Choose an action based on current beliefs."""
        # Decay all beliefs slightly — time has passed
        self.belief.decay(0.95)

        # If uncertain about obstacle, move cautiously
        obs_conf = self.belief.confidences.get("obstacle_ahead", 0.0)
        obstacle = self.belief.get("obstacle_ahead", True)

        if obstacle or obs_conf < 0.6:
            return "scan"           # gather more information first
        return "move_forward"


# Simulate three perception-action cycles
agent = PartiallyObservableAgent("Nav-1")

agent.perceive({"obstacle_ahead": (False, 0.85)})
print(agent.act())    # "move_forward" — confident enough

agent.perceive({"obstacle_ahead": (False, 0.45)})
print(agent.act())    # "scan" — low confidence, gather more data

agent.perceive({"obstacle_ahead": (True, 0.92)})
print(agent.act())    # "scan" — obstacle detected, stop and re-plan
During development and testing it is useful to take a fully specified world state and apply an
to simulate what a sensor with limited range would actually deliver to the agent:
. The environment tracks the ground truth; the agent receives only what the observation function passes through.

In practice, observability exists on a spectrum. A LIDAR sensor in clear weather provides near-full observability of nearby space; in rain or dense fog it degrades toward partial observability. A robust agent should adapt its strategy to the
rather than assuming a fixed observability class:
When the agent receives a new observation, it should update its belief state using
: P(state | observation) ∝ P(observation | state) × P(state). This
is the mathematical foundation of agents that operate in partially observable environments. It formalises how observations shift the probability distribution over possible hidden states:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import random


@dataclass
class SimplePOMDP:
    """
    A minimal Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP).
    Hidden states: ["office", "corridor", "lab"]
    Observations: ["noisy", "quiet"]  (imperfect sensor)
    """

    states: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: ["office", "corridor", "lab"])
    observations: list[str] = field(default_factory=lambda: ["noisy", "quiet"])

    # P(observation | state)  — sensor accuracy matrix
    obs_prob: dict = field(default_factory=lambda: {
        "office":   {"noisy": 0.8, "quiet": 0.2},
        "corridor": {"noisy": 0.5, "quiet": 0.5},
        "lab":      {"noisy": 0.1, "quiet": 0.9},
    })

    def observe(self, true_state: str) -> str:
        """Sample an observation from the sensor model."""
        probs = self.obs_prob[true_state]
        r = random.random()
        cumulative = 0.0
        for obs, p in probs.items():
            cumulative += p
            if r < cumulative:
                return obs
        return list(probs.keys())[-1]

    def belief_update(self, prior: dict[str, float], obs: str) -> dict[str, float]:
        """
        Bayesian belief update: P(s|o) ∝ P(o|s) * P(s).
        Returns a normalised posterior.
        """
        posterior = {}
        for state in self.states:
            posterior[state] = self.obs_prob[state][obs] * prior[state]
        total = sum(posterior.values())
        return {s: p / total for s, p in posterior.items()}


env = SimplePOMDP()
prior = {"office": 1/3, "corridor": 1/3, "lab": 1/3}  # uniform prior

# Receive a "quiet" observation
obs = "quiet"
posterior = env.belief_update(prior, obs)
print({k: round(v, 3) for k, v in posterior.items()})
# {"office": 0.087, "corridor": 0.217, "lab": 0.696}
# "quiet" strongly suggests "lab" — posterior shifts accordingly
— the agent's belief before the observation (uniform = "I have no idea").
— how probable this observation is if the world is in state s (the sensor model).
— updated belief after Bayesian normalisation. This becomes the prior for the next step.

Most production agents do not run exact Bayesian inference — the state space is too large. Instead they use approximations: particle filters, Kalman filters, or neural network-based state estimators. The BeliefState class above is a simplified analogue.

Because the agent's behaviour depends on confidence levels in its belief state, tests should cover the three critical confidence regimes: high confidence (act decisively), medium confidence (plan carefully), and low confidence (gather information first):

import pytest


class TestBeliefState:
    def test_update_stores_value_and_confidence(self):
        bs = BeliefState()
        bs.update("pos", (3, 4), 0.9)
        assert bs.get("pos") == (3, 4)
        assert bs.confidences["pos"] == pytest.approx(0.9)

    def test_decay_reduces_all_confidences(self):
        bs = BeliefState()
        bs.update("a", 1, 1.0)
        bs.update("b", 2, 1.0)
        bs.decay(0.5)
        assert bs.confidences["a"] == pytest.approx(0.5)
        assert bs.confidences["b"] == pytest.approx(0.5)

    def test_most_uncertain_returns_lowest_confidence_key(self):
        bs = BeliefState()
        bs.update("x", 1, 0.9)
        bs.update("y", 2, 0.3)
        assert bs.most_uncertain() == "y"


class TestPartiallyObservableAgent:
    def test_low_confidence_triggers_scan(self):
        agent = PartiallyObservableAgent("test-agent")
        agent.perceive({"obstacle_ahead": (False, 0.4)})   # low confidence
        assert agent.act() == "scan"

    def test_high_confidence_clear_path_moves_forward(self):
        agent = PartiallyObservableAgent("test-agent")
        agent.perceive({"obstacle_ahead": (False, 0.95)})  # high confidence
        assert agent.act() == "move_forward"

    def test_obstacle_detected_triggers_scan(self):
        agent = PartiallyObservableAgent("test-agent")
        agent.perceive({"obstacle_ahead": (True, 0.99)})
        assert agent.act() == "scan"
. Each stage can fail independently, and mixing them in a single end-to-end test makes failures hard to locate.
Every real sensor introduces noise. A

captures the probability distribution P(observation | true_state). This model is what lets the agent interpret noisy readings as evidence about the hidden world state — the noisier the sensor, the less each reading shifts the belief:
Design implications when building agents for noisy sensors:
— averaging five noisy readings is better than trusting one. Confidence grows with evidence.
— an overconfident sensor model assumes less noise than exists, causing the agent to act on false certainty.
— GPS (high accuracy for position), vision (moderate for object detection), inertial (drifts over time for velocity).
A key strategy in partially observable environments is to treat
information gathering as a first-class action
. Instead of always moving toward the goal, a well-designed agent periodically pauses to scan, query, or re-read sensors — especially when confidence decays below a useful threshold:
— scan when average belief confidence falls below SCAN_THRESHOLD. Prevents acting on stale information.
— even if confidence is acceptable, scan every SCAN_INTERVAL steps. Catches drift that slow decay misses.
— only execute goal-directed moves when there is enough certainty to make them worthwhile.
Information-gathering actions are expensive (they take time and don't advance the goal). The design tension is between
(act on current beliefs to reach the goal) and
(gather data to improve beliefs). This is the same trade-off that appears in reinforcement learning.
Agent ignores uncertainty; crashes when state differs from last known
Add belief state; check confidence before acting
Agent acts on stale information from many steps ago
Apply decay each step; trigger re-scan below threshold
Agent scans every step, never makes progress
Bayesian update makes belief worse than prior
Calibrate P(obs|state) to match real sensor noise

Fully observable environments expose the complete world state to the agent at each step, enabling simple, memoryless designs. Partially observable environments hide some state, forcing the agent to maintain a belief state, incorporate uncertainty into its decisions, and sometimes take explicit actions to gather missing information. Correctly classifying observability before designing an agent prevents underbuilt agents (those that ignore hidden state) and overengineered ones (those that maintain unnecessary memory when full state is available).
